speaker: interviewer
text: The date is November 7th, 1996. This is an interview with survivor Marianna Glazek, [BACKGROUND NOISE] last name at birth-- Seeman. The interviewer is Susan Schreiber. And it is taking place in Ann Arbor, Michigan, United States of America. And the language of the interview is English. The date is November 7th, 1996. We are in Ann Arbor, Michigan. My name is Susan Schreiber. And I have the privilege of interviewing Marianna Glazek. Could you please tell us your name?

speaker: interviewee
text: My name is Marianna Glazek.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you spell that?

speaker: interviewee
text: The first name? Both. First name is M-A-R-I-A-N-N-E-- or-- or A. And Glazek is G-L-A-Z-E-K.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your name at birth?

speaker: interviewee
text: Was Seemann, the last name-- S-E-E-M-A-N-N.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when is your birth date?

speaker: interviewee
text: February 10th, 1922.

speaker: interviewer
text: Which would make you how old?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I have to calculate that one-- 75, 74.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where were you born?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was born in Hungary-- Budapest, Hungary.

speaker: interviewer
text: And-- you were-- what was your family like? What are your earliest recollections?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, my earliest recollections-- there were two-- two children in the family, my-- my sister and I. My father was a doctor. And we had our-- our-- we lived in the same place where he had his office. So we always lived on the main street right on the-- what they call a ring. It's a big part of-- of-- of the city. It's a circle in the city. And when we were little, when we were smaller, we had a-- a-- a governess. And then later, we had a German governess. And so we learned German and Hungarian at the same time. And when we were-- we lived in a apartment house-- not like here. These people live in family houses. We lived in apartment house. And we went every day out for a walk to-- to the park. And there, the-- we were sitting-- the governess was sitting and waiting for us. And we were talking with the other ones. And we were playing. Then later, I started school. Then we-- we didn't have a governess anymore. But a lady came in every day to take us for a walk in the afternoon. And when we were even older like that, then somebody came who talked English to us. So we learned Hungarian, German, and English all at the same time. What was the language spoken in your home? Hungarian. It was Hungarian.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were your parents both from Budapest?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, they both were born in Budapest. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And grandparents?

speaker: interviewee
text: They also were born in Budapest. They-- they were all Hungarian, actually. My grandparents-- I really didn't know much of my grandparents. My grandfather, my father's side, had a small grocery store. And when I went to school, I went by the store, and somebody came to pick me up and took me across the street. And then I walked by myself to school at that time. Children walked alone to school. And when-- and my other grandfather got married the second time. And he married his wife's sister. This was kind of a-- a custom in Jewish families that they married the sister who was also-- whose husband also died. And so I just I remember going to her once in a while. She lived on the Danube in a beautiful apartment. And on New Year's-- on St. Stephen's Day, when we have the-- the fireworks, we went to her house, and we watched the fireworks. So every-- every St. Stephen's Day was spelled with watching the fireworks-- except one time. She had a-- she had a parrot. Papa got a parrot. And the parrot bit the maid, I think. The maid collapsed. And I was afraid that I must have done something. I never told anybody until they called the maid, and they realized that she wasn't around. She was collapsed. It was pretty scary kind of thing. And-- and so my other grandfather also remarried. He-- the family was very much against it. So he didn't really-- we didn't really meet his wife very much. But he came to us every Saturday afternoon, visited my father. And that was usually on Saturday afternoon. And my mother was-- insisted on sewing when he was coming. She knew that that's going to irritate him a lot. So she just did it. And then he died later, too. My-- my grandfather, my mother's father, when his wife died, he moved in with us. And he took actually my parents' bedroom. And they lived in-- they stayed in the dining room. And I got another room. So it was quite complicated.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was it a large apartment?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, it was very-- it was quite large. It was the apartment-- it had an office, a doctor's office. And it had a reception room. That was also the doctor's reception room. So those two rooms we really didn't use, and then into the dining room. And actually, my parents slept there. They-- and then it had one bedroom or two bedrooms-- one bedroom. And then there was another bedroom at the end of the hall. This was actually the maid's room. But then when my grandfather moved in with us, she was sleeping somewhere in the kitchen. They fixed up a room for her. And I slept in the maid's room.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why would it irritate your grandfather that your mother was sewing? Because it was the Sabbath, and we weren't supposed to sew. And my mother was not very religious. And she just liked to do it to irritate him, I guess. And every Saturday, he said, do you have to do that today? Can't you do it some other time? Of course, she could have done it. But she didn't want to. Was-- was your family religious?

speaker: interviewee
text: We were-- we were religious in the way that-- at school I went to-- and everybody had to have religion classes in school. So I went to the Jewish religion class. And we-- and we went with the school-- sometimes we went to the synagogue on Friday night. And on the big holidays-- Yom Kippur and-- and New Year-- we-- we went to synagogue. But we were not-- we were not kosher or anything like this. So he-- my grandfather never ate at our house, as far as I remember.

speaker: interviewer
text: He was religious?

speaker: interviewee
text: He was religious, yeah. But that was my father's father, not my mother's father.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was your family political?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the way my father belonged to the Social Democratic Club. That was considered quite political. That was about it. But I think everybody in that club was Jewish. In Hungary, there was not much anti-Semitism-- there was anti-Semitism. But it was not so-- so apparent. And most places where I went or my family went, there were only Jews. The Jews were very separate from-- from gentiles. It was not really openly, but it was more a subtle-- subtle kind of way. And so even my friends were-- most of them were gentile-- were Jewish. They were-- really, in school, I-- there were gentile students, and we were very friendly with each other, but not privately.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was-- was your neighborhood Jewish?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no. We didn't have-- we actually didn't have a Jewish neighborhood. Maybe just Orthodox Jews did.

speaker: interviewer
text: What [INTERPOSING VOICES] part of Budapest did you live in?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was the main part. They have what they call-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] like circles, ring. I don't know what they call-- what they call them in English. But it's a big circle that goes around town. And those where the stores are, and the main streets are there. And so we lived on one of the main streets, very close.

speaker: interviewer
text: As a child, do you remember experiencing any kind of anti-Semitism?

speaker: interviewee
text: As a child? Yes, [INAUDIBLE] it was-- we kind of grew up with it. Certain places we didn't go. We were not invited to big-- to balls or so on or big dances. And certain-- we were not invited to any place that is especially reserved for-- for gentiles. And that-- we knew that, and it-- nobody ever really talked about it. But it-- it was like common knowledge, general knowledge, that you had a certain place where you stayed. Excuse me. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] That was one-- just one part of-- of anti-Semitism that was subtle. Actually, it wasn't so subtle. It's just that we knew our place, and we didn't try to force ourselves on anybody else. Of course, as a child, I never did it very much, either. But my father belonged to a club that was a doctor's club. And they met every day in the coffee house. The coffee house was a very important gathering place. And in summer, they met every day at a-- in a swimming pool on the Danube. This was also very important. And they-- and they discussed medical things there. But I don't think that any gentile doctors belonged to that. So it-- it was quite-- it was not very mixed.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then do you remember when things started to change in Hungary?

speaker: interviewee
text: I-- I can't remember exactly that it changed very much, because Hungary was one of the last countries that got influenced by-- by the Nazism. And so the style of life didn't really change. But we heard all kind of rumors. And lots of people, actually, already left the country and-- and immigrated to Israel or to the United States. But I did not notice anything as far as anti-Semitism in everyday life-- just maybe a few.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did-- did any of your family leave?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, no. My family didn't leave. But that was already taught. The aunt, when I told my father that the family I have been teaching-- I-- I used to teach. My-- my childhood, I went away and gave private lessons to students and helped them with the school work. And [? that ?] my family was-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] was not involved in that. But I told my father that-- that the family I was teaching, that they are planning to leave.

speaker: interviewer
text: In fact, in a few days, they also left. And they went to Israel. And I asked him, you know, why don't we go? And he said, why should we go? He said his-- his patients are all here. And he's used to the people here. He wouldn't be happy somewhere else. What year would you say that was?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was already in-- in '43. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: It must have been '43. And you had-- by '43, you had completed schooling? Or what?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no. I-- I completed-- let's see. No, in '43 I did. I completed schooling in 1940. And so this must have been earlier, then. Because at one point in '36-- in '36 I went with that family, where was tutoring, to Switzerland. They wanted me to come, because when more people went, they got more money.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we went there. And I had to come back, like in the middle of it, because there were some problems. And that, in 1930-- and that was the year when there was the Anschluss. That's the Austrian-- Austrians asked the Germans to come in to the country and take over. You had-- That was in '36, I think. You had gone with the family that you were tutoring to Switzerland?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, to Switzerland. And then I came-- I came back in the middle of the vacation. But they were also-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] and that was the year when-- when Austria was taken over by Germany.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we knew what was going on. But we always thought that all this won't come to Hungary. I don't know why we thought that, but that was like-- we were really convinced of that. When did you first hear about it coming to Hungary? Not to Budapest, but-- With whole-- slowly. You know, it approached Hungary. Austria was just like three hours from-- or like Vienna was three hours from Budapest. So we knew that something was going on there.

speaker: interviewee
text: And so we knew that-- that something is going to happen. But we didn't believe it. It's very hard to explain how-- how that was. I remember-- I  went to a German school. And I-- I remember that the German children went to-- were called to the assembly room. And they had like a celebration, because they had this Anschluss. Austrians called the Germans in, and the Germans actually took over everything in Austria. And so they were celebrating. And that was the first time that I really felt very deeply depressed, so to say, and felt that it's like a shadow that is coming closer and closer to us all the time. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Actually, that was '38, I think.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then what happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I still went to school. And I finished. In 1940, I graduated. And then afterwards, I understand later, I found out, that my sister graduated two years later. And everybody-- and that was about the last year that the students openly graduated. Because in 1940, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Hungary joined the-- the-- the people who were against-- who were against the Allies, were with Germany. And-- but they didn't have to go in yet. And all this in Hungary went very slowly. And my sister graduated in 1942. And that time, it was already quite apparent that the Germans are taking over everything in Hungary, too. They actually did not come into Hungary. They did not take Hungary as their own until 1944. They were kind of allowing Hungary to do it on their own. Hungary was always a very anti-Semitic country. But this time, it was even more anti-Semitic.

speaker: interviewer
text: In-- in what ways?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, there were lots of laws that they brought against Jews going to the university. Even before, the university was a kind of a privilege for most Jews. They only took what they called [NON-ENGLISH]. Just so many Jews were admitted [INAUDIBLE] university. So after 1940, there was what they called [NON-ENGLISH]. No Jews were admitted to university. And then many-- many professions, also they would not even take any Jews. So most Jews tried to take something, to do something that was done by hand, that was like a craft. Because they thought they going to survive this Nazi terror just with a skill of their hands-- the skill of their-- skin of their teeth, so to say. And they-- they learned how to do certain things that they were not very good at. I learned how to do leather work. And I went out and did some leather work. I could not go-- and I graduated in 1940 but couldn't go on to university afterward. So from then on, my life was just like waiting for something to happen. And every day from then on, every day you-- the main purpose of life was to escape danger, to escape being taken into custody, to being taken into-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] taken by the Germans, taken by the Hungarians. So you lived in a country that was-- an enemy of yours. You-- you were an enemy of the country that you were considered to be their ally. My father in the First World War was a lieutenant in the army, because he was a doctor. And so he was a lieutenant in the army. The Second World War, he couldn't have even have dreamt to get any kind of position in the army. My father was so proud of being a Hungarian that when a German-- when a-- a woman, a German woman came to-- a peasant woman to sell us some eggs-- because around Hungary, there were several German settlements where people were still talking German, in spite of the fact that they were born in Hungary. So they were coming and selling eggs. And my father, who usually did not go out in the kitchen, went out in the kitchen and asked the woman why she doesn't talk Hungarian. She said she doesn't know how. And he said if he-- he asked if she's not ashamed not to be able to talk Hungarian in spite of the fact that  she lives in Hungary. And that was a time when I realized that he was very much a Hungarian at that point, I guess. Then in 1940, things started falling apart. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I don't even know who they-- who they took into the army. But a lot of young-- they started taking people in the country, who were living in the country, they started taking them and shipping them out of the country, putting them in labor camps, in forced labor camps, and make them work-- do the real dirty work there, like ammunition and-- and being the first in the line in setting up bombs and everything-- I mean, explosives. And so we heard that they were transporting a lot of people out of the country, out of the cities, and transporting them somewhere into the country. But nothing came to Budapest here. So we were like a privileged place, where you kind of lived like you lived before, if you had enough money, of course. But we were not attacked. Or we were not shipped out.

speaker: interviewer
text: So it-- that was until 1940. Then in 1941 and 1942, it actually started. And they were shipping people out from Hungary, from Budapest, not only from Hungary. And we heard about all those villages that were taken to Auschwitz and what happened to them.

speaker: interviewee
text: Actually, we did not really get any official notification what happened to them. We were-- just heard about things that-- that was done to them, that they shipped them in cattle cars. And some people found pieces of paper where people who were shipped in those cattle cars wrote that they were going, they were moving out, and they were going out, and they didn't know what to do. They did-- they write-- they wrote they are all right. But they were not really all right. And things started getting pretty scary. So from then on, the main thing was to keep alive. That was the only, main thing. And that's what we were mostly concerned with. My father still kept his-- his office. And we still lived in the same place until 1944. Then in 1944, the Germans got pretty upset that nothing happens in Hungary. Everything was way too slow and especially in Budapest. I understand that they didn't get the carts, that they were going to the cattle cars that they were supposed to get to ship everybody. And things started to get pretty eerie. And so that's when a lot of people left. And my father absolutely refused to move.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you still living at home in 1944?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, in '44, I was still-- I guess. No, I got married-- I was married in 1942. I got married, and then we-- that's right. And then we-- I moved out to my own-- to our own apartment. And my parents stayed in the-- in their old house.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I-- I remember asking my father, why don't we leave? And he said, well, his patients know him there. He can't just leave everybody. And we never did leave.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then in 1944, I lived by my-- by my husband-- 1943, 1944. My husband was called up for labor to-- to go into forced labor camp. But he-- he-- he went. And he stayed around town. Luckily, he stayed around town, and when he could, he came home. And that's when things started to get really bad.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who was your husband?

speaker: interviewee
text: My second--

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did you--

speaker: interviewee
text: This was my first husband. He was a-- he was weaving. He worked in a factory that made material-- ribbons. He couldn't go to-- to school. So he learned it all there and had a very good job. They were making narrow textiles, so to say. And that's where he was working. And he seemed to be-- it was also a Jewish factory. And he seemed to be doing pretty well, until he was called in and had to leave his job and so on. So that was pretty good.

speaker: interviewer
text: How-- how old a man was he?

speaker: interviewee
text: He was just three years older than I was. We met each other when I was 14 and he was 17. And we've been going together ever since. My parents didn't know. First my father wouldn't let me go with anybody. He thought I was too young. And it was not the kind of marriage he wanted me to have. He wanted me to have a doctor, I guess. But at that time, it didn't make much difference anyway.

speaker: interviewer
text: We'll go on to tape number 2.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK.
